macro_segments.title: Sale of land with explicit boundaries: tolerance for measurement error and the meaning of 'up to a sixth'

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the case: selling land by explicit boundaries versus by acreage estimate
The Mishnah's language: 'less than a sixth' (pochos mishtus) and 'up to a sixth' (ad shtus) — ambiguity in determining whether a full sixth is tolerable
Rav Huna's interpretation: a sixth is like less than a sixth and therefore tolerable; Rav Yehuda's counter-interpretation: a sixth is like more than a sixth and must be corrected
Analysis of the two readings: עד (ad) as inclusive or exclusive; how the Mishnah language can support either position

macro_segments.title: Comparison to judges' assessments and the Braita's apparent contradiction about sixths

micro_segments.title: The Braita: 'if one goes over or under by a sixth, it is like the assessment of judges' — and the problem that judges' assessments are voided at a sixth
Rav Huna's resolution: the Braita only partially compares to judges (the sixth is the relevant threshold), but the outcome is opposite (tolerable here, voided there)

macro_segments.title: The case of Rav Papa's land purchase: when the buyer knew the actual acreage and cannot claim an adjustment

micro_segments.title: Rav Papa bought land claimed to be twenty units but measured at only fifteen; he sought adjustment from Abaye
Abaye's ruling: 'you assessed and accepted' (savar v'kabil) — since you knew the acreage, you cannot later claim adjustment based on the seller's claim
The Rashbam's explanation: the buyer's silence and acceptance of a price based on the seller's (false) claim does not entitle him to later correction

macro_segments.title: Division of inheritance among brothers by drawing lots and the moment when division becomes final

micro_segments.title: Introduction: brothers inherit an estate with multiple plots and divide by drawing lots; the question is whether one brother drawing a lot suffices or all must draw
The Mishnah: once one brother draws his lot, all brothers acquire their portions (keivan she'ala goral l'echad mehem kanu kulam)
Rabbi Elazar's reason: the lot division is like the division of Eretz Yisrael (by lots); the question of whether other methods of division would work
Rav Ashi's addition: even without the full apparatus of Urim v'Tumim, the brothers' agreement and intent (gmiras da'at) suffices in place of the ritual
The Rashbam's reading: Rav Ashi's answer modifies (not replaces) Rabbi Elazar's reason; the model of Eretz Yisrael remains fundamental

macro_segments.title: A third brother arrives from overseas: what happens to the division already made by the first two brothers?

micro_segments.title: The case: two brothers divided the estate by lot; then a third brother emerges with proof of kinship and claims his share
Rava's position: the entire division is voided (batla machlokas) and must restart with three equal shares
Shmuel's position: 'carve out' (mekamtzim) — keep the existing division fixed and allocate the third brother's portion by reducing the first two brothers' shares proportionally
The Rashbam and Tosafot on fairness: concerns about whether carving out leaves the third brother with unequal or suboptimal land; Tosafot's suggestion of a new draw to preserve fairness

macro_segments.title: Comparison to a case of two partners dividing without the third and the principle of holding a judgment final (kam dina) even when there is error

micro_segments.title: Two of three partners divide the estate without the third partner; the question whether this division stands if the third partner shows up

Distinction: if all three knew at the outset there were three, then the division was made with that assumption and stands (no mistake); if only two were thought to exist, the division was made in error and is void
Rav Pappa's challenge to Abaye: if kam dina (a judgment stands), then why must the entire division be re-done rather than kept as valid where possible?
Parallel to the case of selling a kor for thirty units but receiving only twenty-nine: Shmuel's logic that a partial acquisition should remain valid

The Gemara's answer: unlike a structured sale (which allows either party to retract until completion), a division among brothers requires 'things that are pleasing to the seller and buyer' — hence intent matters and innocent error voids the whole

topical_tags.term: Pochos mishtus (less than a sixth) vs. ad shtus (up to a sixth) — ambiguity in measurement tolerance
Shum hadayanim (judges' assessment) — sixths and voiding
Savar v'kabil (assessed and accepted) — knowledge bars later claims for adjustment
Gorall (drawing lots) — method of dividing inherited property
Kinyan (acquisition) — formal act required for transfer
Gmiras da'at (full intent, willingness) — suffices for division even without ritual apparatus
Chalukas Eretz Yisrael (division of the land of Israel) — model for lot-based division
Batla machlokas (division is voided) — response to discovering a third heir
Mekamtzim (carving out) — preserving most of existing division while accommodating new heir
Kam dina (a judgment stands) — principle that valid acts should not be fully reversed
Rav Huna vs. Rav Yehuda — dispute over whether a full sixth is tolerable
Rav Papa — sold land, measured short, cannot claim adjustment due to savar v'kabil
Rava vs. Shmuel (on third heir) — void all vs. carve out
Rashbam — interpretation and reconciliation of tensions in the Gemara
Tosafot — fairness objection to carving out; proposal for new lot draw
Chatzipin, chatlukin (division) and keivan she'ala goral (once one draws)
Urim v'Tumim (priestly lots) — formal apparatus for dividing Eretz Yisrael
Beis din (court) — role in overseeing divisions and assessments (implied in Rashbam)
Kiddushei mikveh (acquiring by lot) — partial vs. full acquisition
